_purpose: Courthouse Atlas for Lafayette Parish, Louisiana. Record survival, courthouse history, and known losses for the commercial and cultural capital of Acadiana. Lafayette Parish was created in 1823 from St. Martin Parish and holds continuous records from the 1820s, with earlier records at St. Martinville. The parish's records document the transition from Cajun cattle-and-subsistence economy to the oil boom capital of southwestern Louisiana.

current_courthouse.location: Lafayette, LA -- 800 South Buchanan Street. Modern courthouse in downtown Lafayette.

previous_courthouses.location: Vermilionville (now Lafayette) -- frame courthouse near the Bayou Vermilion crossing. Lafayette Parish was originally called Lafayette County (renamed to parish under the 1845 constitution).
previous_courthouses.fate: Replaced by a more substantial structure as the settlement grew.

previous_courthouses.location: Lafayette -- brick courthouse on Jefferson Street
previous_courthouses.fate: Served the parish through the antebellum, Civil War, and Reconstruction eras. Records transferred to successor building.

previous_courthouses.location: Lafayette -- Romanesque Revival courthouse on Jefferson Street that became a regional landmark
previous_courthouses.fate: Replaced by the current modern courthouse. The old courthouse was demolished. Records transferred intact.

record_losses.cause: Parish creation -- Lafayette was carved from St. Martin Parish in 1823
records_survived: Pre-1823 records remain at St. Martin Parish Clerk of Court, St. Martinville
record_losses.description: Lafayette Parish was created in 1823 from the northwestern portion of St. Martin Parish. All records predating 1823 for the Vermilionville (Lafayette) area reside at the St. Martin Parish Clerk of Court in St. Martinville. This includes colonial-era Attakapas district records, early Acadian settlement documents, and Spanish colonial land grants. St. Martinville was the social and administrative capital of Acadiana before Lafayette surpassed it commercially.
record_losses.narrative_impact: If {ancestor_name}'s family settled in the Vermilionville area before 1823, their land grants, marriage records, and successions are at St. Martinville -- the old Acadian capital on Bayou Teche, not the newer commercial center on Bayou Vermilion.
record_losses.source: Griffin, Harry Lewis. The Attakapas Country: A History of Lafayette Parish, Louisiana. New Orleans: Pelican, 1959.

record_losses.cause: Civil War -- Federal operations in the Teche Campaign
records_destroyed: Some loose Confederate-era papers and militia records
record_losses.description: Federal forces moved through the Lafayette area during the Teche campaigns of 1863. The courthouse was not destroyed, but the disruption of civil government created gaps in contemporary record-keeping. Some Confederate-era militia records and administrative papers were lost. The bound record books survived.
record_losses.narrative_impact: The Civil War's impact on Lafayette Parish records was administrative disruption rather than physical destruction. Records from 1863-1865 may show gaps in routine civil proceedings while the bound historical records survived.
record_losses.source: Winters, John D. The Civil War in Louisiana. Baton Rouge: LSU Press, 1963.

deeds.completeness: Good -- continuous conveyance records from parish creation. Some early conveyances are in French, reflecting the overwhelmingly Francophone population.
deeds.gaps: Pre-1823 conveyances at St. Martin Parish. The transition from French to English in conveyance records occurred gradually through the 1840s-1860s, with some documents bilingual.
deeds.repository: Lafayette Parish Clerk of Court, Lafayette
deeds.online_access: Some indexes through Louisiana Secretary of State; Lafayette Parish Clerk online portal

wills_estates.completeness: Good -- succession records from parish creation. Under Louisiana's Napoleonic Code, successions include detailed inventories of community property (biens de la communaute) that are exceptionally valuable for genealogy.
wills_estates.gaps: Pre-1823 successions at St. Martin Parish. Early Lafayette successions are in French.
wills_estates.repository: Lafayette Parish Clerk of Court, Lafayette

marriages.completeness: Good from parish creation. Catholic sacramental records at St. John the Evangelist Cathedral (founded 1821) supplement and sometimes predate civil records.
marriages.gaps: Pre-1823 civil marriages at St. Martin Parish. Church records at St. Martin de Tours in St. Martinville cover the area from the 1760s.
marriages.repository: Lafayette Parish Clerk of Court; Diocese of Lafayette archives
marriages.online_access: Some marriage indexes at Louisiana Secretary of State

court_minutes.completeness: Good -- continuous from parish creation. Early minutes include Police Jury proceedings documenting road building, levee maintenance, and cattle brand registration.
court_minutes.gaps: Some gaps during Federal occupation 1863-1865. Pre-1823 court records at St. Martin Parish.
court_minutes.repository: Lafayette Parish Clerk of Court, Lafayette

vital_records.completeness: Fair -- compliance improved after 1910. Catholic baptismal and burial records are more complete for the early period.
vital_records.repository: Louisiana Office of Vital Records, New Orleans; Lafayette City-Parish Health Unit

substitute_sources.description: All civil records for the Lafayette area before 1823 reside at the St. Martin Parish Clerk of Court in St. Martinville. This includes the earliest Attakapas district records, Spanish colonial documents, and the foundational Acadian settlement records.
substitute_sources.repository: St. Martin Parish Clerk of Court, St. Martinville, LA
substitute_sources.genealogical_value: Essential for tracing Acadian families to their arrival in the Attakapas district. The St. Martinville records include land grants, church records from St. Martin de Tours (one of the oldest Catholic churches in Acadiana), and colonial administrative documents in French and Spanish.
substitute_sources.source: Brasseaux, Carl A. The Founding of New Acadia. Baton Rouge: LSU Press, 1987.

substitute_sources.source_type: Catholic sacramental records -- St. John the Evangelist Cathedral
substitute_sources.description: St. John the Evangelist Cathedral in Lafayette (founded 1821) maintains sacramental registers from its founding. Earlier records for the Lafayette area are at St. Martin de Tours in St. Martinville (records from the 1760s).
substitute_sources.repository: St. John the Evangelist Cathedral, Lafayette; Diocese of Lafayette archives
substitute_sources.genealogical_value: Catholic records are the backbone of Acadian genealogy. Baptismal records name godparents, marriage records name parents, and burial records note family relationships. The shift from French to English in church records occurred later than in civil records.
substitute_sources.source: Hebert, Donald J. Southwest Louisiana Records. Eunice, LA: self-published, 1974-2000.

substitute_sources.source_type: University of Louisiana at Lafayette -- Center for Louisiana Studies and Dupre Library Special Collections
substitute_sources.description: The university holds extensive manuscript collections for Acadiana, including family papers, plantation records, business records, and oral history collections. The Center for Louisiana Studies has published primary source compilations essential for regional genealogy.
substitute_sources.repository: University of Louisiana at Lafayette, Dupre Library, Lafayette, LA
substitute_sources.genealogical_value: A research institution with deep Acadiana collections. The oral history program has recorded first-person accounts from Cajun and Creole families that provide context unavailable in written records.
substitute_sources.source: Center for Louisiana Studies, University of Louisiana at Lafayette.

narrative_hooks: Lafayette Parish's records begin in 1823, but {ancestor_name}'s Acadian roots in the Attakapas district go back to the 1760s -- and those earliest records are at St. Martinville, the old Acadian capital where the exiles from Nova Scotia first gathered.

The succession inventories at Lafayette's courthouse divide community property according to the Napoleonic Code -- a legal system inherited from France that treated marriage as an economic partnership, listing every pot, plow, and parcel that {ancestor_name}'s family owned.

If {ancestor_name}'s family registered a cattle brand in Lafayette Parish, it appears in the Police Jury minutes -- a mark as unique as a signature, burned into the hides of the open-range cattle that were the prairie Cajun's primary wealth.

Catholic church records at St. John the Evangelist Cathedral in Lafayette name {ancestor_name}'s godparents -- parrains and marraines chosen with the same care as business partners, because in Cajun Acadiana, they were.
source_references: Hebert, Donald J. Southwest Louisiana Records. 39 vols. Eunice, LA: self-published, 1974-2000.
Griffin, Harry Lewis. The Attakapas Country: A History of Lafayette Parish, Louisiana. New Orleans: Pelican, 1959.
Brasseaux, Carl A. The Founding of New Acadia. Baton Rouge: LSU Press, 1987.
Winters, John D. The Civil War in Louisiana. Baton Rouge: LSU Press, 1963.
Center for Louisiana Studies, University of Louisiana at Lafayette.
